sources.name: 2023 GINA Report - Global Strategy for Asthma Management

patient.communicationStyle: Speaks in short phrases due to dyspnea. Knows her baseline and can compare. Has been through exacerbations before but says this one is worse than usual. Cooperative and wants to participate in management decisions.
patient.emotionalState: Anxious, task-focused on breathing, trusts the system

responses.opening: I'm having an asthma attack... (pause to breathe) ...and it's not getting better with my inhaler. I've used my rescue inhaler... (pause) ...maybe 8 times today and it's barely helping. I can't talk in full sentences right now. This is worse than my usual flares.
responses.severity: It's bad... (pause) ...maybe 7 out of 10. I can't walk to the bathroom... (pause) ...without getting completely winded. Normally my rescue inhaler brings me back to baseline pretty fast.
responses.onset: It started two days ago... (pause) ...after I was around my friend's cat. I'm allergic to cats. I took my inhaler and Benadryl but it kept getting worse. Last night was really bad... (pause) ...and this morning I couldn't even eat breakfast without getting short of breath.
responses.triggers: The cat was the trigger this time. (pause) My allergies have been bad this week too — lots of pollen. And I ran out of my Flovent about a week ago... (pause) ...I was going to refill it but didn't get to the pharmacy.
responses.medications: I take Flovent twice a day and... (pause) ...albuterol as needed. I also take Singulair. But I've been out of the Flovent for about a week.
responses.priorExacerbations: I've been to the ER maybe 3 or 4 times... (pause) ...in the past couple years. I've needed prednisone bursts maybe twice a year. I've never been intubated... (pause) ...and I've never been in the ICU for it.

chiefComplaint.onset: Gradual over 2 days, worsened significantly overnight. Triggered by cat exposure + concurrent pollen season + ICS non-adherence (ran out 1 week ago).
chiefComplaint.duration: Progressive for 48 hours, now at peak
chiefComplaint.severity: Cannot speak in full sentences. Speaking in phrases. Rescue inhaler minimally effective.

present.name: wheezing (bilateral)
present.patientSays: I can hear myself wheezing. My chest is really tight.

present.name: dyspnea (progressive)
present.patientSays: I'm working so hard to breathe. I can't take a deep breath.

present.name: chest tightness
present.patientSays: It feels like a band around my chest.

present.name: cough (dry → slightly productive)
present.patientSays: I've been coughing a lot, mostly dry but some thick mucus.

present.name: accessory muscle use
present.patientSays: (Visible — patient sitting tripod, using neck muscles)

present.name: nasal congestion/rhinorrhea
present.patientSays: My allergies have been terrible this week too.

present.name: poor sleep
present.patientSays: I barely slept last night. Kept waking up wheezing.

absent.name: purulent sputum
absent.patientSays: No, the mucus is clear, just thick.

absent.name: orthopnea (prior to this episode)
absent.patientSays: No, I normally sleep flat. It's just during flares.

pmh: Persistent moderate asthma (diagnosed age 8, currently on Step 3 therapy — ICS-LABA should be considered given exacerbation frequency)
Allergic rhinitis (perennial — dust mites, cats; seasonal — tree pollen)
2-3 ED visits for asthma per year
No prior intubations, no prior ICU admissions

medications.name: Fluticasone (Flovent) 110mcg
medications.patientSays: I ran out about a week ago and haven't gotten it refilled.

medications.name: Albuterol MDI
medications.patientSays: I've been using it all day. It helps a little for maybe 20 minutes then it wears off.

medications.name: Montelukast (Singulair)
medications.patientSays: I take this every night. I'm current on this one.

socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives with roommate (no pets). Visited friend with cats 2 days ago.
socialHistory.environmentalExposures: Carpeted apartment, uses HEPA filter. No mold. No cockroach exposure. No secondhand smoke.
socialHistory.exerciseTolerance: Baseline: can run 2-3 miles. Current: can't walk across the room.

present: ICS non-adherence (ran out of Flovent 1 week ago — strongest modifiable risk factor for exacerbation)
family history of severe asthma (brother intubated)
absent: prior intubation (strongest predictor of fatal asthma)

physicalExam.general: Young adult in moderate respiratory distress, sitting upright in tripod position, speaking in phrases (4-5 words between breaths), visibly using accessory muscles
physicalExam.heent: Nasal turbinate edema, clear rhinorrhea. No oral lesion or angioedema.
physicalExam.neck: Sternocleidomastoid and scalene contraction visible. No JVD. Trachea midline.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Pulsus paradoxus 18mmHg (measured — normal <10; >20 suggests severe obstruction).
physicalExam.pulmonary: Diffuse bilateral expiratory wheezes. Prolonged expiratory phase (I:E ratio ~1:3). Decreased air movement at bases. No crackles. No focal consolidation. Using accessory muscles. Audible wheeze without stethoscope.
physicalExam.extremities: No edema, no clubbing, warm and well-perfused
physicalExam.skin: No cyanosis. Mild eczematous patches on antecubital fossae (atopic dermatitis).

peakFlow.interpretation: Moderate-severe exacerbation (40-69% predicted = moderate; <40% = severe). This is at the low end of moderate.

labs.note: Mild hypoxemia. Normal pCO2 is concerning in asthma — should be LOW (hyperventilation). Normal/rising pCO2 = fatigue/impending respiratory failure.
Monitor potassium — albuterol causes intracellular shift, steroids can cause retention

imaging.findings: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms. No focal consolidation or effusion. No pneumothorax. No pneumomediastinum. Heart size normal.
imaging.impression: Hyperinflation consistent with air trapping. No acute cardiopulmonary process to suggest alternative diagnosis.

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: After 3 rounds of duonebs + IV steroids: HR decreases to 92, RR 20, SpO2 96% on RA. Wheezing decreasing.
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Can speak in full sentences after 2 hours. Peak flow improves to 310 L/min (74% predicted).
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: Continuous albuterol nebs x3 then q2h. Ipratropium added for first 3 doses. Methylprednisolone 125mg IV given. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV over 20 minutes. After 2-3 hours of aggressive therapy, patient significantly improved. Transitioned to q4h albuterol. Observed 2 more hours. Discharged with: prednisone 40mg x5 days, refilled Flovent (with spacer technique reviewed), asthma action plan, PCP follow-up in 1 week, allergen avoidance counseling.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: RR increases to 36, SpO2 drops to 88%, pCO2 rises to 48 → impending respiratory failure
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Patient tiring. Wheezing diminishing — but NOT because she's improving. It's because she's moving so little air. This is the ominous 'silent chest.' pCO2 rising. BiPAP or intubation imminent.

keyLearningPoints: Severity assessment: speaks in words (severe) vs phrases (moderate) vs sentences (mild). This patient speaks in phrases = moderate-severe.
Peak flow <40% predicted = severe; 40-69% = moderate. Use it to quantify and track response.

pCO2 in asthma should be LOW (hyperventilation response). A NORMAL pCO2 (35-45) is a red flag — it means the patient is tiring and losing the ability to compensate. Rising pCO2 = impending respiratory failure.
Pulsus paradoxus >10mmHg correlates with severity. >20mmHg = severe obstruction. >25mmHg = consider ICU.
Treatment triad: (1) continuous albuterol nebs, (2) systemic corticosteroids within first hour, (3) ipratropium added to first 3 nebulizer treatments.
IV magnesium sulfate 2g IV over 20 minutes for moderate-severe exacerbations — smooth muscle relaxant, safe, evidence-based.
ICS non-adherence was the precipitant. This is the #1 modifiable risk factor for exacerbations. Always address the controller regimen before discharge.
Discharge checklist: prednisone burst (40mg x 5 days), refill ICS, spacer technique, asthma action plan, allergen avoidance, PCP follow-up.
mustNotMiss: Pneumothorax (sudden worsening, unilateral decreased breath sounds — high risk in status asthmaticus from air trapping)
Anaphylaxis masquerading as asthma (check for urticaria, angioedema, hypotension, allergen exposure)
Foreign body aspiration (unilateral wheeze, no prior asthma history — more relevant in pediatrics/elderly)
Vocal cord dysfunction (inspiratory stridor, laryngeal origin — often misdiagnosed as refractory asthma)
commonPitfalls: Interpreting a normal pCO2 as reassuring — in asthma, it's a danger sign
Hearing 'less wheezing' and assuming improvement — the 'silent chest' means critical air trapping, not resolution
Delaying systemic steroids — they take 4-6 hours to work. Give within the first hour.
Sending home without addressing the controller medication gap — the patient will be back in 2 weeks
Not measuring peak flow before AND after treatment — you need objective data, not just 'she looks better'
Using sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids) for anxiety in actively wheezing patient — respiratory depression risk

expertDifferential.why: Known asthma + identifiable trigger (cat, pollen) + ICS non-adherence + classic presentation
Cough and wheeze overlap, but no fever or URI prodrome
Allergen exposure present (cat), but no urticaria, angioedema, or hypotension
Young female with 'refractory' symptoms, but responding to bronchodilators argues against
No fever, no focal findings, CXR clear

teachingHooks.ifNotChecksABG: You've been treating for an hour. She looks a little better but her RR is still 26. How do you know she's truly improving vs tiring out? What single test tells you?
teachingHooks.ifReassuredByNormalCO2: Her pCO2 is 36 — right in the 'normal' range. But is normal actually normal in asthma? What should it be if she's working this hard to breathe?
teachingHooks.ifDischargesWithoutControllerPlan: She's feeling better and wants to go home. Before you write that discharge — what caused this flare, and what's going to prevent the next one?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutMagnesium: Good question. IV magnesium is evidence-based for moderate-severe exacerbations. It relaxes bronchial smooth muscle independent of beta-agonist pathways. Give 2g IV over 20 minutes.
teachingHooks.ifWantsBiPAP: BiPAP can be a bridge to avoid intubation in select patients, but it doesn't treat the underlying bronchospasm. Make sure you've maximized bronchodilators and steroids first. And if you're thinking about BiPAP — you should also be thinking about calling the ICU.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutStepUpTherapy: Great longitudinal thinking. She's on Step 3 (medium-dose ICS + montelukast) but having 2-3 exacerbations per year. Should she step up to Step 4 (medium-dose ICS-LABA like Advair)? Probably yes. But today's visit isn't the time to adjust the controller — that's for her PCP follow-up. Today, you treat the acute episode and set up the outpatient visit.

patient.communicationStyle: Can only manage single words. Too dyspneic for history. Drowsy appearing — fatigue, not sedation. Nods/shakes head. Family at bedside. This is a critically ill patient — the interview is secondary to resuscitation.
patient.emotionalState: Exhausted, terrified, too breathless to express it

responses.opening: (Family member) He's been wheezing all day. His inhaler ran out and he couldn't afford the refill. He got worse really fast in the last couple hours. He stopped talking about an hour ago — he can't catch his breath. He's had really bad asthma his whole life. He was in the ICU on a breathing machine when he was 14.
responses.patientAttempt: (Single words, gasping between) Can't... breathe... help...
responses.familyAdditional: (Family) He doesn't have insurance right now. He lost his job last month. He's been out of all his medications for about 3 weeks.

chiefComplaint.onset: Progressive dyspnea all day (approximately 12 hours). Acute worsening in last 2 hours. No nebulizer at home. Albuterol MDI ran out. Off all controllers for 3 weeks.
chiefComplaint.collateralHistory: Family reports: severe persistent asthma since childhood, prior intubation at age 14, multiple ICU admissions as a child. Lost insurance 1 month ago, all medications lapsed.

pmh: PRIOR INTUBATION (age 14 — strongest predictor of near-fatal asthma)
Multiple ICU admissions as pediatric patient

medications.name: Fluticasone/salmeterol (Advair) 500/50
medications.dose: 1 puff BID — ran out 3 weeks ago

medications.name: All medications lapsed due to insurance loss

present: depression (comorbid psychiatric illness — associated with poor outcomes)

physicalExam.general: Young adult in SEVERE respiratory distress. Tripod position. Diaphoretic. Speaking in single words only. Drowsy — fatigue from work of breathing. Using all accessory muscles. Retractions visible.
physicalExam.neck: Severe SCM and scalene contraction. Tracheal tug visible.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic. Pulsus paradoxus 28mmHg. Distant heart sounds.
physicalExam.pulmonary: DIMINISHED BREATH SOUNDS BILATERALLY with MINIMAL WHEEZE — 'silent chest.' This is NOT improvement — it reflects critical air trapping with almost no air movement. Severely prolonged expiratory phase. Intercostal and supraclavicular retractions.
physicalExam.neurologic: Drowsy. Responds to voice but with delay. Oriented to name only. This is CO2 narcosis from hypercapnia.

peakFlow.interpretation: Inability to perform peak flow = severe exacerbation by definition

labs.note: Acute respiratory acidosis. pCO2 58 = respiratory muscle fatigue / failure. Hypoxemia despite high-flow O2. This is impending respiratory arrest.
VBG pCO2 >45 in asthma = critical. Correlates within 5mmHg of arterial.
Elevated from respiratory muscle work + hypoxemia + epinephrine effect
Low from continuous albuterol (intracellular K+ shift). Replete to prevent arrhythmia.

imaging.findings: Severe hyperinflation. Flattened diaphragms. No pneumothorax (CRITICAL to verify). No consolidation. Heart appears small (hyperinflation effect).

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: After continuous nebs + IV steroids + IV Mg + epinephrine SQ + ketamine: SpO2 improves to 91%, RR decreases to 28, patient more alert. Avoids intubation.
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: Continuous albuterol nebulization started immediately. Ipratropium added. Methylprednisolone 125mg IV. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV over 20 min. Epinephrine 0.3mg IM given for life-threatening exacerbation. IV access x2. ICU called for bed. Patient placed on BiPAP 12/5 as bridge. After 1 hour, slight improvement — repeat ABG pH 7.33, pCO2 48. Continuing aggressive management. Patient avoids intubation with 4 hours of continuous therapy. ICU admission for close monitoring.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: RR drops to 12 (fatigue — not improvement), SpO2 78%, pCO2 72, pH 7.18
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Patient obtunded. Respiratory effort declining. This is not improvement — it is impending arrest. Emergent intubation required. Asthmatic intubation is extremely high risk: dynamic hyperinflation, peri-intubation cardiac arrest, difficult ventilation post-intubation. Use ketamine for induction (bronchodilator properties). Low rate, long expiratory time on ventilator.

keyLearningPoints: The 'SILENT CHEST' is an emergency — diminishing wheeze means so little air is moving that there's nothing to wheeze. This is NOT improvement.
Rising or normal pCO2 + drowsiness = respiratory muscle fatigue. This patient needs ICU and may need intubation.
Prior intubation for asthma is the single strongest predictor of fatal asthma. This patient's history demands maximum vigilance.
IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5mg) is indicated for life-threatening asthma exacerbation when nebulizers are insufficient — same dose and route as anaphylaxis.

Intubating an asthmatic is EXTREMELY HIGH RISK: dynamic hyperinflation → decreased venous return → PEA arrest. Use ketamine (bronchodilator) for induction, slow respiratory rate, long expiratory time.
Social determinants: This patient lost insurance → lost medications → nearly died. Address medication access on discharge (340B pharmacy, patient assistance programs, emergency Medicaid).
Heliox (helium-oxygen mixture) can reduce airway resistance in severe cases as a bridge to avoid intubation — lower density gas flows more easily through narrowed airways.
IV terbutaline can be used for refractory status asthmaticus when inhaled beta-agonists can't reach distal airways.
mustNotMiss: Pneumothorax (sudden deterioration, unilateral absent breath sounds — high risk with severe air trapping and positive pressure ventilation)
Peri-intubation cardiac arrest (dynamic hyperinflation → decreased preload → PEA. Disconnect from ventilator and allow passive exhalation.)
Hypokalemia from continuous albuterol (arrhythmia risk — replete)
Mucus plugging (sudden complete obstruction of a mainstem bronchus)
teaching.attendingQuestions: This patient has a silent chest, pCO2 of 58, and is becoming drowsy. Is the wheezing getting better? What does this triad tell you? Walk me through your next 15 minutes — what are you doing simultaneously and in what order? And tell me about the social factors that led to this preventable near-death.
